result.markdown:
---
title: "Visualization in mlr3"
description: |
  Quickly plot the mlr3 ecosystem.
categories:
  - visualization
author:
  - name: Marc Becker
    orcid: 0000-0002-8115-0400
    url: https://github.com/be-marc
date: 2022-12-22
---






# Scope

We showcase the visualization functions of the mlr3 ecosystem.
The [mlr3viz](https://mlr3viz.mlr-org.com) package creates a plot for almost all mlr3 objects.
This post displays all available plots with their reproducible code.
We start with plots of the base mlr3 objects.
This includes boxplots of tasks, dendrograms of cluster learners and ROC curves of predictions.
After that, we tune a classification tree and visualize the results.
Finally, we show visualizations for filters.

::: {.callout-note}
This article will be updated whenever a new plot is available in `mlr3viz`.
:::

# Package

The [mlr3viz](https://mlr3viz.mlr-org.com) package defines `autoplot()` functions to draw plots with [ggplot2](https://cran.r-project.org/package=ggplot2).
Often there is more than one type of plot for an object.
You can change the plot with the `type` argument.
The help pages list all possible choices.
The easiest way to access the help pages is via the [pkgdown website](https://mlr3viz.mlr-org.com/reference/index.html).
The plots use the [viridis](https://cran.r-project.org/package=viridis) color pallet and the appearance is controlled with the `theme` argument.
By default, the [`minimal theme`](https://www.rdocumentation.org/packages/ggpplot2/topics/theme_minimal) is applied.

# Tasks

## Classification

We begin with plots of the classification task [`Palmer Penguins`](https://mlr3.mlr-org.com/reference/mlr_tasks.html).
We plot the class frequency of the target variable.


::: {.cell layout-align="center"}

```{.r .cell-code}
library(mlr3viz)
library(mlr3learners)
library(mlr3tuning)
library(mlr3cluster)

task = tsk("penguins")
task$select(c("body_mass", "bill_length"))

autoplot(task, type = "target")
```

::: {.cell-output-display}
![](index_files/figure-html/2022-12-22-mlr3viz-002-1.png){fig-align='center' width=672}
:::
:::


The `"duo"` plot shows the distribution of multiple features.


::: {.cell layout-align="center"}

```{.r .cell-code}
autoplot(task, type = "duo")
```

::: {.cell-output-display}
![](index_files/figure-html/2022-12-22-mlr3viz-003-1.png){fig-align='center' width=672}
:::
:::


The `"pairs"` plot shows the pairwise comparison of multiple features.
The classes of the target variable are shown in different colors.


::: {.cell layout-align="center"}

```{.r .cell-code}
autoplot(task, type = "pairs")
```

::: {.cell-output-display}
![](index_files/figure-html/2022-12-22-mlr3viz-004-1.png){fig-align='center' width=672}
:::
:::


## Regression

Next, we plot the regression task [`mtcars`](https://mlr3.mlr-org.com/reference/mlr_tasks.html).
We create a boxplot of the target variable.


::: {.cell layout-align="center"}

```{.r .cell-code}
task = tsk("mtcars")
task$select(c("am", "carb"))

autoplot(task, type = "target")
```

::: {.cell-output-display}
![](index_files/figure-html/2022-12-22-mlr3viz-005-1.png){fig-align='center' width=672}
:::
:::


The `"pairs"` plot shows the pairwise comparison of mutiple features and the target variable.


::: {.cell layout-align="center"}

```{.r .cell-code}
autoplot(task, type = "pairs")
```

::: {.cell-output-display}
![](index_files/figure-html/2022-12-22-mlr3viz-006-1.png){fig-align='center' width=672}
:::
:::


## Cluster

Finally, we plot the cluster task [`US Arrests`](https://mlr3.mlr-org.com/reference/mlr_tasks.html).
The `"pairs"` plot shows the pairwise comparison of mutiple features.


::: {.cell layout-align="center"}

```{.r .cell-code}
library(mlr3cluster)

task = mlr_tasks$get("usarrests")

autoplot(task, type = "pairs")
```

::: {.cell-output-display}
![](index_files/figure-html/2022-12-22-mlr3viz-007-1.png){fig-align='center' width=672}
:::
:::


# Learner

## Classification

The `"prediction"` plot shows the decision boundary of a classification learner and the true class labels as points.


::: {.cell layout-align="center"}

```{.r .cell-code}
task = tsk("pima")$select(c("age", "pedigree"))
learner = lrn("classif.rpart")
learner$train(task)

autoplot(learner, type = "prediction", task)
```

::: {.cell-output-display}
![](index_files/figure-html/2022-12-22-mlr3viz-008-1.png){fig-align='center' width=672}
:::
:::


Using probabilities.


::: {.cell layout-align="center"}

```{.r .cell-code}
task = tsk("pima")$select(c("age", "pedigree"))
learner = lrn("classif.rpart", predict_type = "prob")
learner$train(task)

autoplot(learner, type = "prediction", task)
```

::: {.cell-output-display}
![](index_files/figure-html/2022-12-22-mlr3viz-009-1.png){fig-align='center' width=672}
:::
:::


## Regression

The `"prediction"` plot of a regression learner illustrates the decision boundary and the true response as points.


::: {.cell layout-align="center"}

```{.r .cell-code}
task = tsk("california_housing")$select("median_income")
learner = lrn("regr.rpart")
learner$train(task)

autoplot(learner, type = "prediction", task)
```

::: {.cell-output-display}
![](index_files/figure-html/2022-12-22-mlr3viz-010-1.png){fig-align='center' width=672}
:::
:::


When using two features, the response surface is plotted in the background.


::: {.cell layout-align="center"}

```{.r .cell-code}
task = tsk("california_housing")$select(c("latitude", "longitude"))
learner = lrn("regr.rpart")
learner$train(task)

autoplot(learner, type = "prediction", task)
```

::: {.cell-output-display}
![](index_files/figure-html/2022-12-22-mlr3viz-011-1.png){fig-align='center' width=672}
:::
:::


## GLMNet

The [`classification`](https://mlr3learners.mlr-org.com/reference/mlr_learners_classif.glmnet.html) and [`regression`](https://mlr3learners.mlr-org.com/reference/mlr_learners_regr.glmnet.html)  GLMNet learner is equipped with a plot function.


::: {.cell layout-align="center"}

```{.r .cell-code}
library(mlr3data)

task = tsk("ilpd")
task$select(setdiff(task$feature_names, "gender"))
learner = lrn("classif.glmnet")
learner$train(task)

autoplot(learner, type = "ggfortify")
```

::: {.cell-output-display}
![](index_files/figure-html/2022-12-22-mlr3viz-012-1.png){fig-align='center' width=672}
:::
:::



::: {.cell layout-align="center"}

```{.r .cell-code}
task = tsk("mtcars")
learner = lrn("regr.glmnet")
learner$train(task)

autoplot(learner, type = "ggfortify")
```

::: {.cell-output-display}
![](index_files/figure-html/2022-12-22-mlr3viz-013-1.png){fig-align='center' width=672}
:::
:::


## Rpart

We plot a [`classification tree`](https://mlr3.mlr-org.com/reference/mlr_learners_classif.rpart.html) of the [rpart](https://cran.r-project.org/package=rpart) package.
We have to fit the learner with `keep_model = TRUE` to keep the model object.


::: {.cell layout-align="center"}

```{.r .cell-code}
task = tsk("penguins")
learner = lrn("classif.rpart", keep_model = TRUE)
learner$train(task)

autoplot(learner, type = "ggparty")
```

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stderr}

```
Warning in ggparty::geom_edge_label(): Ignoring unknown parameters: `label.size`
```


:::

::: {.cell-output-display}
![](index_files/figure-html/2022-12-22-mlr3viz-014-1.png){fig-align='center' width=672}
:::
:::


We can also plot regression trees.


::: {.cell layout-align="center"}

```{.r .cell-code}
task = tsk("mtcars")
learner = lrn("regr.rpart", keep_model = TRUE)
learner$train(task)

autoplot(learner, type = "ggparty")
```

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stderr}

```
Warning in ggparty::geom_edge_label(): Ignoring unknown parameters: `label.size`
```


:::

::: {.cell-output-display}
![](index_files/figure-html/2022-12-22-mlr3viz-015-1.png){fig-align='center' width=672}
:::
:::


## ClustHierachical

The `"dend"` plot shows the result of the hierarchical clustering of the data.


::: {.cell layout-align="center"}

```{.r .cell-code}
library(mlr3cluster)

task = tsk("usarrests")
learner = lrn("clust.hclust")
learner$train(task)

autoplot(learner, type = "dend", task = task)
```

::: {.cell-output-display}
![](index_files/figure-html/2022-12-22-mlr3viz-016-1.png){fig-align='center' width=672}
:::
:::


The `"scree"` type plots the number of clusters and the height.


::: {.cell layout-align="center"}

```{.r .cell-code}
autoplot(learner, type = "scree")
```

::: {.cell-output-display}
![](index_files/figure-html/2022-12-22-mlr3viz-017-1.png){fig-align='center' width=672}
:::
:::


# Prediction

## Classification

We plot the predictions of a classification learner.
The `"stacked"` plot shows the predicted and true class labels.


::: {.cell layout-align="center"}

```{.r .cell-code}
task = tsk("spam")
learner = lrn("classif.rpart", predict_type = "prob")
pred = learner$train(task)$predict(task)

autoplot(pred, type = "stacked")
```

::: {.cell-output-display}
![](index_files/figure-html/2022-12-22-mlr3viz-018-1.png){fig-align='center' width=672}
:::
:::


The ROC curve plots the true positive rate against the false positive rate at different thresholds.


::: {.cell layout-align="center"}

```{.r .cell-code}
autoplot(pred, type = "roc")
```

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stderr}

```
Warning in ggplot2::fortify(object, raw_curves = raw_curves, reduce_points = reduce_points): Arguments in `...` must be used.
✖ Problematic argument:
• raw_curves = raw_curves
ℹ Did you misspell an argument name?
```


:::

::: {.cell-output-display}
![](index_files/figure-html/2022-12-22-mlr3viz-019-1.png){fig-align='center' width=672}
:::
:::



The precision-recall curve plots the precision against the recall at different thresholds.


::: {.cell layout-align="center"}

```{.r .cell-code}
autoplot(pred, type = "prc")
```

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stderr}

```
Warning in ggplot2::fortify(object, raw_curves = raw_curves, reduce_points = reduce_points): Arguments in `...` must be used.
✖ Problematic argument:
• raw_curves = raw_curves
ℹ Did you misspell an argument name?
```


:::

::: {.cell-output-display}
![](index_files/figure-html/2022-12-22-mlr3viz-020-1.png){fig-align='center' width=672}
:::
:::


The `"threshold"` plot varies the threshold of a binary classification and plots against the resulting performance.


::: {.cell layout-align="center"}

```{.r .cell-code}
autoplot(pred, type = "threshold")
```

::: {.cell-output-display}
![](index_files/figure-html/2022-12-22-mlr3viz-021-1.png){fig-align='center' width=672}
:::
:::


## Regression

The predictions of a regression learner are often presented as a scatterplot of truth and predicted response.


::: {.cell layout-align="center"}

```{.r .cell-code}
task = tsk("california_housing")
learner = lrn("regr.rpart")
pred = learner$train(task)$predict(task)

autoplot(pred, type = "xy")
```

::: {.cell-output-display}
![](index_files/figure-html/2022-12-22-mlr3viz-022-1.png){fig-align='center' width=672}
:::
:::


Additionally, we plot the response with the residuals.


::: {.cell layout-align="center"}

```{.r .cell-code}
autoplot(pred, type = "residual")
```

::: {.cell-output-display}
![](index_files/figure-html/2022-12-22-mlr3viz-023-1.png){fig-align='center' width=672}
:::
:::


We can also plot the distribution of the residuals.


::: {.cell layout-align="center"}

```{.r .cell-code}
autoplot(pred, type = "histogram")
```

::: {.cell-output-display}
![](index_files/figure-html/2022-12-22-mlr3viz-024-1.png){fig-align='center' width=672}
:::
:::


## Cluster

The predictions of a cluster learner are often presented as a scatterplot of the data points colored by the cluster.


::: {.cell layout-align="center"}

```{.r .cell-code}
library(mlr3cluster)

task = tsk("usarrests")
learner = lrn("clust.kmeans", centers = 3)
pred = learner$train(task)$predict(task)

autoplot(pred, task, type = "scatter")
```

::: {.cell-output-display}
![](index_files/figure-html/2022-12-22-mlr3viz-025-1.png){fig-align='center' width=672}
:::
:::


The `"sil"` plot shows the silhouette width of the clusters.
The dashed line is the mean silhouette width.


::: {.cell layout-align="center"}

```{.r .cell-code}
autoplot(pred, task, type = "sil")
```

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stderr}

```
Warning: Using `size` aesthetic for lines was deprecated in ggplot2 3.4.0.
ℹ Please use `linewidth` instead.
ℹ The deprecated feature was likely used in the ggfortify package.
  Please report the issue at <https://github.com/sinhrks/ggfortify/issues>.
```


:::

::: {.cell-output-display}
![](index_files/figure-html/2022-12-22-mlr3viz-026-1.png){fig-align='center' width=672}
:::
:::


The `"pca"` plot shows the first two principal components of the data colored by the cluster.


::: {.cell layout-align="center"}

```{.r .cell-code}
autoplot(pred, task, type = "pca")
```

::: {.cell-output-display}
![](index_files/figure-html/2022-12-22-mlr3viz-027-1.png){fig-align='center' width=672}
:::
:::


# Resample Result

## Classification

The `"boxplot"` shows the distribution of the performance measures.


::: {.cell layout-align="center"}

```{.r .cell-code}
task = tsk("sonar")
learner = lrn("classif.rpart", predict_type = "prob")
resampling = rsmp("cv")
rr = resample(task, learner, resampling)

autoplot(rr, type = "boxplot")
```

::: {.cell-output-display}
![](index_files/figure-html/2022-12-22-mlr3viz-028-1.png){fig-align='center' width=672}
:::
:::


We can also plot the distribution of the performance measures as a "`histogram`".


::: {.cell layout-align="center"}

```{.r .cell-code}
autoplot(rr, type = "histogram")
```

::: {.cell-output-display}
![](index_files/figure-html/2022-12-22-mlr3viz-029-1.png){fig-align='center' width=672}
:::
:::


The ROC curve plots the true positive rate against the false positive rate at different thresholds.


::: {.cell layout-align="center"}

```{.r .cell-code}
autoplot(rr, type = "roc")
```

::: {.cell-output-display}
![](index_files/figure-html/2022-12-22-mlr3viz-030-1.png){fig-align='center' width=672}
:::
:::


The precision-recall curve plots the precision against the recall at different thresholds.


::: {.cell layout-align="center"}

```{.r .cell-code}
autoplot(rr, type = "prc")
```

::: {.cell-output-display}
![](index_files/figure-html/2022-12-22-mlr3viz-031-1.png){fig-align='center' width=672}
:::
:::


The `"prediction"` plot shows two features and the predicted class in the background.
Points mark the observations of the test set and the color presents the truth.


::: {.cell layout-align="center"}

```{.r .cell-code}
task = tsk("pima")
task$filter(seq(100))
task$select(c("age", "glucose"))
learner = lrn("classif.rpart")
resampling = rsmp("cv", folds = 3)
rr = resample(task, learner, resampling, store_models = TRUE)

autoplot(rr, type = "prediction")
```

::: {.cell-output-display}
![](index_files/figure-html/2022-12-22-mlr3viz-032-1.png){fig-align='center' width=672}
:::
:::


Alternatively, we can plot class probabilities.


::: {.cell layout-align="center"}

```{.r .cell-code}
task = tsk("pima")
task$filter(seq(100))
task$select(c("age", "glucose"))
learner = lrn("classif.rpart", predict_type = "prob")
resampling = rsmp("cv", folds = 3)
rr = resample(task, learner, resampling, store_models = TRUE)

autoplot(rr, type = "prediction")
```

::: {.cell-output-display}
![](index_files/figure-html/2022-12-22-mlr3viz-033-1.png){fig-align='center' width=672}
:::
:::


In addition to the test set, we can also plot the train set.


::: {.cell layout-align="center"}

```{.r .cell-code}
task = tsk("pima")
task$filter(seq(100))
task$select(c("age", "glucose"))
learner = lrn("classif.rpart", predict_type = "prob", predict_sets = c("train", "test"))
resampling = rsmp("cv", folds = 3)
rr = resample(task, learner, resampling, store_models = TRUE)

autoplot(rr, type = "prediction", predict_sets = c("train", "test"))
```

::: {.cell-output-display}
![](index_files/figure-html/2022-12-22-mlr3viz-034-1.png){fig-align='center' width=672}
:::
:::


The `"prediction"` plot can also show categorical features.


::: {.cell layout-align="center"}

```{.r .cell-code}
task = tsk("german_credit")
task$filter(seq(100))
task$select(c("housing", "employment_duration"))
learner = lrn("classif.rpart")
resampling = rsmp("cv", folds = 3)
rr = resample(task, learner, resampling, store_models = TRUE)

autoplot(rr, type = "prediction")
```

::: {.cell-output-display}
![](index_files/figure-html/2022-12-22-mlr3viz-035-1.png){fig-align='center' width=672}
:::
:::


## Regression

The "`prediction`" plot shows one feature and the response.
Points mark the observations of the test set.


::: {.cell layout-align="center"}

```{.r .cell-code}
task = tsk("california_housing")
task$select("median_income")
task$filter(seq(100))
learner = lrn("regr.rpart")
resampling = rsmp("cv", folds  = 3)
rr = resample(task, learner, resampling, store_models = TRUE)

autoplot(rr, type = "prediction")
```

::: {.cell-output-display}
![](index_files/figure-html/2022-12-22-mlr3viz-036-1.png){fig-align='center' width=672}
:::
:::


Additionally, we can add confidence bounds.


::: {.cell layout-align="center"}

```{.r .cell-code}
task = tsk("california_housing")
task$select("median_income")
task$filter(seq(100))
learner = lrn("regr.lm", predict_type = "se")
resampling = rsmp("cv", folds  = 3)
rr = resample(task, learner, resampling, store_models = TRUE)

autoplot(rr, type = "prediction")
```

::: {.cell-output-display}
![](index_files/figure-html/2022-12-22-mlr3viz-037-1.png){fig-align='center' width=672}
:::
:::


And add the train set.


::: {.cell layout-align="center"}

```{.r .cell-code}
task = tsk("california_housing")
task$select("median_income")
task$filter(seq(100))
learner = lrn("regr.lm", predict_type = "se", predict_sets = c("train", "test"))
resampling = rsmp("cv", folds = 3)
rr = resample(task, learner, resampling, store_models = TRUE)

autoplot(rr, type = "prediction", predict_sets = c("train", "test"))
```

::: {.cell-output-display}
![](index_files/figure-html/2022-12-22-mlr3viz-038-1.png){fig-align='center' width=672}
:::
:::


We can also add the prediction surface to the background.


::: {.cell layout-align="center"}

```{.r .cell-code}
task = tsk("california_housing")
task$select(c("latitude", "longitude"))
task$filter(seq(100))
learner = lrn("regr.rpart")
resampling = rsmp("cv", folds = 3)
rr = resample(task, learner, resampling, store_models = TRUE)

autoplot(rr, type = "prediction")
```

::: {.cell-output-display}
![](index_files/figure-html/2022-12-22-mlr3viz-039-1.png){fig-align='center' width=672}
:::
:::


# Benchmark Result

We show the performance distribution of a benchmark with multiple tasks.


::: {.cell layout-align="center"}

```{.r .cell-code}
tasks = tsks(c("pima", "sonar"))
learner = lrns(c("classif.featureless", "classif.rpart", "classif.xgboost"), predict_type = "prob")
resampling = rsmps("cv")
bmr = benchmark(benchmark_grid(tasks, learner, resampling))

autoplot(bmr, type = "boxplot")
```

::: {.cell-output-display}
![](index_files/figure-html/2022-12-22-mlr3viz-040-1.png){fig-align='center' width=672}
:::
:::


We plot a benchmark result with one task and multiple learners.


::: {.cell layout-align="center"}

```{.r .cell-code}
tasks = tsk("pima")
learner = lrns(c("classif.featureless", "classif.rpart", "classif.xgboost"), predict_type = "prob")
resampling = rsmps("cv")
bmr = benchmark(benchmark_grid(tasks, learner, resampling))
```
:::


We plot an roc curve for each learner.


::: {.cell layout-align="center"}

```{.r .cell-code}
autoplot(bmr, type = "roc")
```

::: {.cell-output-display}
![](index_files/figure-html/2022-12-22-mlr3viz-042-1.png){fig-align='center' width=672}
:::
:::


Alternatively, we can plot precision-recall curves.


::: {.cell layout-align="center"}

```{.r .cell-code}
autoplot(bmr, type = "prc")
```

::: {.cell-output-display}
![](index_files/figure-html/2022-12-22-mlr3viz-043-1.png){fig-align='center' width=672}
:::
:::


# Tuning Instance

We tune the hyperparameters of a decision tree on the sonar task.
The `"performance"` plot shows the performance over batches.


::: {.cell layout-align="center"}

```{.r .cell-code}
library(mlr3tuning)
library(mlr3tuningspaces)
library(mlr3learners)

instance = tune(
  tuner = tnr("gensa"),
  task = tsk("sonar"),
  learner = lts(lrn("classif.rpart")),
  resampling = rsmp("holdout"),
  measures = msr("classif.ce"),
  term_evals = 100
)

autoplot(instance, type = "performance")
```

::: {.cell-output-display}
![](index_files/figure-html/2022-12-22-mlr3viz-044-1.png){fig-align='center' width=672}
:::
:::


The `"incumbent"` plot shows the performance of the best hyperparameter setting over the number of evaluations.


::: {.cell layout-align="center"}

```{.r .cell-code}
autoplot(instance, type = "incumbent")
```

::: {.cell-output-display}
![](index_files/figure-html/2022-12-22-mlr3viz-045-1.png){fig-align='center' width=672}
:::
:::



The `"parameter"` plot shows the performance for each hyperparameter setting.


::: {.cell layout-align="center"}

```{.r .cell-code}
autoplot(instance, type = "parameter", cols_x = c("cp", "minsplit"))
```

::: {.cell-output-display}
![](index_files/figure-html/2022-12-22-mlr3viz-046-1.png){fig-align='center' width=672}
:::
:::


The `"marginal"` plot shows the performance of different hyperparameter values.
The color indicates the batch.


::: {.cell layout-align="center"}

```{.r .cell-code}
autoplot(instance, type = "marginal", cols_x = "cp")
```

::: {.cell-output-display}
![](index_files/figure-html/2022-12-22-mlr3viz-047-1.png){fig-align='center' width=672}
:::
:::


The `"parallel"` plot visualizes the relationship of hyperparameters.


::: {.cell layout-align="center"}

```{.r .cell-code}
autoplot(instance, type = "parallel")
```

::: {.cell-output-display}
![](index_files/figure-html/2022-12-22-mlr3viz-048-1.png){fig-align='center' width=672}
:::
:::


We plot `cp` against `minsplit` and color the points by the performance.


::: {.cell layout-align="center"}

```{.r .cell-code}
autoplot(instance, type = "points", cols_x = c("cp", "minsplit"))
```

::: {.cell-output-display}
![](index_files/figure-html/2022-12-22-mlr3viz-049-1.png){fig-align='center' width=672}
:::
:::


Next, we plot all hyperparameters against each other.


::: {.cell layout-align="center"}

```{.r .cell-code}
autoplot(instance, type = "pairs")
```

::: {.cell-output-display}
![](index_files/figure-html/2022-12-22-mlr3viz-050-1.png){fig-align='center' width=672}
:::
:::


We plot the performance surface of two hyperparameters.
The surface is interpolated with a learner.


::: {.cell layout-align="center"}

```{.r .cell-code}
autoplot(instance, type = "surface", cols_x = c("cp", "minsplit"), learner = mlr3::lrn("regr.ranger"))
```

::: {.cell-output-display}
![](index_files/figure-html/2022-12-22-mlr3viz-051-1.png){fig-align='center' width=672}
:::
:::


# Filter

We plot filter scores for the mtcars task.


::: {.cell layout-align="center"}

```{.r .cell-code}
library(mlr3filters)

task = tsk("mtcars")
f = flt("correlation")
f$calculate(task)

autoplot(f, n = 5)
```

::: {.cell-output-display}
![](index_files/figure-html/2022-12-22-mlr3viz-052-1.png){fig-align='center' width=672}
:::
:::


# Conclusion

The [mlr3viz](https://mlr3viz.mlr-org.com) package brings together the visualization functions of the mlr3 ecosystem.
All plots are drawn with the `autoplot()` function and the appearance can be customized with the `theme` argument.
If you need to highly customize a plot e.g. for a publication, we encourage you to check our code on [GitHub](https://github.com/mlr-org/mlr3viz).
The code should be easily adaptable to your needs.
We are also looking forward to new visualizations.
You can suggest new plots in an issue on [GitHub](https://github.com/mlr-org/mlr3viz/issues).
